title: Harvard Engineers Develop Tunable Chip for Real-Time Light Chirality Control
summary: Researchers at Harvard's School of Engineering and Applied Sciences have developed a compact device capable of actively controlling the chirality, or handedness, of light in real time. This device uses a twisted bilayer photonic crystal, where two engineered layers can be rotated relative to each other using a micro-electromechanical system (MEMS). Chirality refers to the property where objects, like light, cannot be superimposed on their mirror images, similar to left and right hands. In optics, light can have left- or right-circular polarization, and this chip can distinguish and adjust between them. This advancement is significant because chirality plays a critical role in various scientific fields, such as chemistry and medicine, where molecules with different chirality can have vastly different effects, as seen with drugs like thalidomide. The tunable nature of the device, achieved through MEMS control of twist angle and spacing, allows for continuous adjustment without replacing parts, enabling new applications in chiral sensing, optical communication, and quantum photonics. The research, led by graduate student Fan Du and professor Eric Mazur, builds on concepts from twistronics and demonstrates improved selectivity in light manipulation.

the direction in which light waves vibrate, with circular polarization referring to light spinning clockwise or counter-clockwise

background_read: Chirality is a fundamental concept in science, describing asymmetry where an object and its mirror image are not identical, akin to left and right hands. In optics, light exhibits chirality through circular polarization, spinning either clockwise or counter-clockwise. This property is crucial in fields like chemistry and medicine, as molecules with different chirality can interact differently with biological systems; for instance, thalidomide had beneficial and harmful effects based on its chiral forms. Traditional tools for studying chirality, such as wave plates, are limited because they are static. The Harvard device advances this by using photonic crystals—nanoscale materials that manipulate light—combined with twistronics, a method inspired by twisted graphene layers. By integrating MEMS for real-time adjustment, it offers tunable control, enabling more precise research and applications in sensing, communication, and quantum technologies, highlighting the intersection of physics, engineering, and biomedical science.
Article_Structure: Main Points: The device uses twisted bilayer photonic crystals with MEMS control to actively manipulate light chirality in real time, enabling tunable selectivity for left- and right-circular polarization. Purpose: To advance chiral sensing, optical communication, and quantum photonics by providing a flexible platform that overcomes limitations of static tools. Evidence Evaluation: The study, published in Optica, demonstrates experimental results showing near-perfect selectivity, supported by concepts from twistronics and prior research on graphene. Author Credibility: Led by Eric Mazur, a professor of physics and applied physics, and graduate student Fan Du at Harvard SEAS, known for expertise in photonics and nanomaterials. Methodology: Involves designing and testing a reconfigurable device using engineered silicon nitride layers, MEMS for precise control, and optical measurements to assess chirality manipulation.

perspectives.description: The device represents a leap in photonic engineering, offering real-time tunability that could revolutionize applications in science and industry.
